segments.s329-g17: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed at the clause start. گاو آن is the noun phrase 'that cow' (subject), and زیر چرم اندر است is a locative predicate: زیر ('under') + چرم ('the hide/skin') with the poetic preposition/locative اندر ('in/within') and the copula است ('is'). Word order is Topic (adverb) – Subject – Locative predicate.
segments.s329-g18: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). پاداش و بادافره is a coordinated noun phrase ('reward and بادافره') serving as the subject of the verbless predicate. دیگرست is an archaic/pseudo-copular predicate meaning 'is different/other' (third‑person singular); in poetry the singular predicate can follow a compound subject. The clause gives the reason or contrast for the previous statement.
segments.s329-g19: بترسید is a second‑person plural (or polite) imperative meaning 'fear' (or 'revere/stand in awe of'). یکسر is an adverb modifying the imperative ('entirely/completely'), and ز یزدان پاک ('of/from the pure God') is the object introduced by the poetic preposition ز — together: 'fear the pure God entirely.'
segments.s329-g20: مباشید is the negative imperative 'do not be' (second‑person plural). ایمن is a predicative adjective 'safe/secure'. بدین is the prepositional/locative expression (poetic contraction of به + این, 'in/with this') introducing the place/condition, followed by تیره خاک 'dark/ominous earth' as its complement. Note: the tokenization here splits بدین into ب + دین; دین as listed does not mean 'religion' in this construction but forms part of the locative بدین (see explanation).
segments.s329-g21: که is a subordinating conjunction 'for/since/that' introducing the clause. این روز is the subject 'this day'; بر ما is a prepositional phrase 'over us/on us'; همی + بگذرد uses the classical present form (همی + present stem) giving an ongoing or imminent sense often translated as 'will pass' or 'still passes' (third‑person singular). The clause states that the present day will pass over us.
segments.s329-g22: زمانه is the subject 'time/fate' (here meaning fortune/time). دم هر کسی is the object phrase 'the moment/breath of each person' (دم = moment/breath, هر کسی = every person). بشمرد is third‑person singular present/subjunctive of شمردن 'to count/reckon' and here means 'counts/reckons' (in effect 'will reckon' or 'takes account of'). Word order places subject (زمانه) before object and verb.
segments.s329-g23: ز is the preposition 'from' introducing a list of names. هوشنگ و جمشید و کاووس are coordinated proper names joined by و ('and'); شاه follows as a title ('king') attached to the list, either appositive to the last name or as a title applying to the group in classical poetic style.
segments.s329-g24: که introduces a relative clause 'who'. بودند is the past plural copula 'they were'. با introduces a list of possessions: فر (the mythical royal radiance/glory, 'farr'), و تخت ('and throne'), و کلاه ('and crown' or head‑gear). The clause attributes these royal attributes to the named kings.
segments.s329-g41: به ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the Iranians' (به + plural noun). بخشم is first-person present/optative of بخشیدن ('I grant / I will grant'); the subject 'I' is omitted as typical in Persian. این خواسته is the direct object 'this request/desired thing' placed after the verb here by poetic word order.
segments.s329-g42: سلیح is the noun 'weapon' and is being described by the following adjectival/locative phrase. و simply links the noun phrase to the locative phrase. در گنج آراسته is a participial/adjectival phrase 'adorned in the treasury' (در + گنج as location, آراسته is a past-participle used adjectivally), placed after the head noun in the normal Persian noun‑then‑modifier order.
segments.s329-g43: هر آن کس که هست is a relative clause 'whoever / each person who is'; کس is the indefinite noun, که introduces the clause, and هست is the copula 'is'. از شما is a partitive 'from among you / of you', and مهتری is the predicate nominal 'a chief/elder' placed at the end as in Persian predicative constructions.
segments.s329-g44: ببخشم is again first-person present/optative 'I will give/grant'. به هر مهتری is the indirect object 'to each chief' (به + هر + noun), and کشوری functions substantively here as 'a realm/kingdom' (an object or complement) — literal order 'to each chief (a) realm'.
segments.s329-g45: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same/those' pointing to items already referred to. بدره is a poetic word meaning 'full-moon' used metaphorically for beautiful persons (here 'moonlike beauties'); برده is 'slave'; چارپای is 'quadruped' or 'beast of burden'. The three nouns form a coordinated list.
segments.s329-g46: براندیشم is a first-person verb 'I will consider / I will deliberate' (present/future sense). آرم here follows as a second verb coordinated with the first (a short poetic sequence of verbs). شمارش به جای is the object phrase 'the counting/reckoning in its place' (شمارش 'counting', به جای 'in place' / 'in their proper place'), so the clause means 'I will consider them and set their count in place.'
segments.s329-g47: ببخشم is first-person present/optative 'I give / I grant'. که introduces a reason/explanatory clause 'for / because'. من is the explicit subject 'I' (added for emphasis); راه را is the definite object 'the road/the way' with را marking it; ساختم is past tense 'I built/made', so the clause reads 'because I built the way.'
segments.s329-g48: وز is here the fused form of و + از meaning 'and from' (a common poetic contraction). این تیرگی دل is the noun phrase 'this darkness of the heart' (این + تیرگی 'this darkness', دل 'heart' in genitive relation). بپرداختم is a past-tense first-person verb meaning 'I dealt with / I removed / I set aright' (here used to mean driving away or dispelling), so the whole clause says 'and I drove this darkness from my heart.'
segments.s329-g57: نَدَانَم (ندانم) is an archaic/poetic negative present: 'I do not know' (literally 'not-I-know'). بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon him'; the following بر چه is a wh‑phrase ('what upon') and خواهد رسید is a future periphrasis (خواهد + past stem رسید) meaning 'will reach/occur'. The repeated بر marks the relation 'upon' for او and introduces the wh‑constituent.
segments.s329-g58: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where'. خواهد آرمید is the same future periphrasis (خواهد + آرمید) meaning 'will rest' or 'will be laid to rest'. این تاج و تخت is the noun phrase (this crown and throne) serving as the theme/subject whose fate is being asked about; و simply coordinates تاج and تخت.
segments.s329-g59: برفتند is the simple past (preterite) third‑person plural 'they went/they departed'. یکسر is an adverb here meaning 'at once' / 'altogether'. گروهاگروه is a reduplicated compound meaning 'groups and groups' or 'group after group' and modifies how they departed.
segments.s329-g60: همه is a distributive/collective determiner 'the whole' or 'all'. The following words form a loose appositive/list of things (or people/forces) associated with the departure: دشت 'plain', لشکر 'army/host'. بد و راغ و کوه are coordinated items (joined by و) — here presented as nouns/adjectival nouns ('the bad/evil, the desirous/longing, and the mountain') forming part of the catalogue; و is the coordinating 'and'.
segments.s329-g61: غو نای is a genitive/attributive sequence where غو 'uproar/tumult' is qualified by نای 'the ney (reed‑flute)': 'the tumult of the ney'. آوای مستان is likewise 'the voice/song of the revelers'. ز (poetic for از) + دشت means 'from the plain', marking source. The sequence lists sounds coming from the plain.
segments.s329-g62: تو 'you' is a parenthetical/addressing pronoun here (as in 'you said'). گفتی is used in a parenthetical sense 'you said' (the supplied senses list treats it as 'talk/discussion'); همی is an older adverb meaning 'continually' / 'kept on'. از هوا برگذشت is a verbal phrase where برگذشت (past stem with no auxiliary here) means 'passed by' or 'moved through' and از هوا 'through the air' marks the medium.
segments.s329-g63: ببودند is an older/perfective form meaning 'they were' or 'they remained', used here to state a continued condition. یک هفته is a duration ('for a week'). ز این‌گونه 'of this kind' / 'like this' modifies شاد 'merry, happy'. Word order places the time expression and manner phrase after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s329-g64: کسی را نیامد is an impersonal/idiomatic construction meaning 'it did not come to anyone' → 'no one …'. را marks the person affected; نیامد is past negative 'did not come' (or 'did not occur to'). غم و رنج یاد is the thing that 'did not come' — 'the memory/remembrance of sorrow and suffering' (یاد + غم و رنج). Overall: 'no one remembered sorrow and suffering.'
segments.s329-g25: This clause expresses an exception followed by an existential verb: 'جز' (except) opens the phrase 'جز از نام' ('except for the name'), then 'از ایشان' marks the possessor/source ('of them'), 'به گیتی' ('in/into the world') gives the locus, and 'نماند' is the past third-person singular of ماندن used existentially ('remained' / 'was left'). Word order (exception → possessor → location → verb) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s329-g26: 'کسی' here is used negatively ('no one'). 'نامه رفتگان' is a genitive construction: 'نامه' = record/document and 'رفتگان' = 'the departed' (the possessors). 'برنخواند' is a literary/archaic negative verbal form (بر + خواند) meaning 'does not / did not read' or 'will not read' depending on context; with the negative indefinite 'کسی' the sense is 'no one reads (the records)'.
segments.s329-g27: The phrase begins with the preposition 'از' marking direction/target ('toward them' / 'of them'), followed by 'بسی' (classical adverb meaning 'much' / 'many') and the adjective 'ناسپاسان' ('ungrateful, thankless'). 'بدند' is the literary past/copular form 'they were'. Together: 'many were ungrateful toward them.'
segments.s329-g28: 'به فرجام' ('concerning/at the outcome') gives the topic; 'زآن' ('from/for that' or 'because of that') introduces the cause; 'بد' qualifies 'فرجام' as 'bad/unfavorable'; 'هراسان' is a predicative adjective 'frightened'; and 'بدند' again is the past third-person plural copula 'they were'. The clause means 'they were much frightened because of that bad outcome.'
segments.s329-g29: 'چو' is the comparative/similar particle 'like/as'. 'ایشان همان' = 'just like them' or 'as for them (the same)'; 'من یکی' literally 'I one' = 'I am one' (i.e. one person); 'بنده‌ام' is the copular construction 'I am a bende(=servant)' – here a humble self-reference common in literary Persian. The whole reads 'Like them, I am one (single) humble servant.'
segments.s329-g30: 'وگر' (concessive 'and if / but if / yet') + 'چند' (classical concessive/limited: 'though few' / 'although') introduces a concessive clause. 'با رنج' ('with toil/suffering') marks instrument/condition, and 'کوشنده‌ام' is the present participle + copula 'I am striving' ('I strive' / 'I am an effort-making one'). Together: 'and yet, though small in number, I strive with hardship.'
segments.s329-g31: 'بکوشیدم' is the past first-person singular perfect/imperfect of کوشیدن: 'I strove/I tried.' 'و رنج بردم' uses بردن with رنج to mean 'I bore/endured suffering.' 'بسی' is an adverb meaning 'much/very' modifying the extent of suffering. The clause lists actions the speaker performed: 'I tried and suffered much.'
segments.s329-g32: Main verb 'ندیدم' ('I did not see') introduces a subordinate content clause with 'که' ('that/which'): 'که ایدر بماند کسی' = 'that anyone remain here.' 'ایدر' is the adverb 'here'; 'بماند' is the (present/jussive/literary) third-person singular of ماندن ('remain'); 'کسی' is indefinite 'anyone.' The whole means 'I never saw anyone remain here.'
segments.s329-g1: شهنشاه is the subject 'the emperor' andنشست is the simple past verb 'sat' (he sat); the prepositional phrase بر تخت زرین 'on the golden throne' sits between subject and verb. زرین is an adjective following the noun تخت (typical Persian noun + adjective order).
segments.s329-g2: This is a nominal/pphrase describing an object in the ruler's hand: یکی 'one/a certain' + گرزه 'mace' with the adjective گاوپیکر 'cow‑bodied / ox‑like' following it; به دست is a locative phrase 'in (his) hand' (literary). The clause lacks an explicit verb because it apposes/describes the scene.
segments.s329-g3: به یک دست here means 'on one side / in one (of his) hands' (literary locative). او is the pronoun 'he' (the king); the names زال و رستم are listed as companions and به هم 'together' (به + هم acting adverbially). The clause is nominal (no finite verb) and describes the arrangement of people around the king.
segments.s329-g4: چو is the simile marker 'like/as'; پیل 'elephant' and سرافراز 'proud/lofty/triumphant' form the first image, و links to شیر 'lion' which is qualified by دژم 'grim/fierce'. The phrase modifies the preceding list (they are compared to these animals).
segments.s329-g5: به دست دگر = 'on the other side / in the other hand' (دگر = 'other'). The string lists proper names طوس و گودرز و گیو as those placed on that side. Again this is a nominal descriptive phrase (no overt verb).
segments.s329-g6: دگر at the start = 'other/another'; this segment lists more names (بیژن) followed by گرد and رهام نیو, which here are name‑elements/epithets. The phrase is a continuation of the catalogue of heroes; word‑order is noun(s) in sequence without a linking verb.
segments.s329-g7: نهاده is the past participle/preterite of نهادن 'to place' used here in a nominal/verbal participial phrase 'having placed / placed'; همه چهر literally 'all faces' = 'all of them (in person)/all presented themselves'. بر چشم شاه is a poetic locative 'before the king's eye' meaning 'in the king's presence'.
segments.s329-g8: بدان is be- + دان (be‑ as prefix + دانستن 'to know') and with تا introduces the purpose 'in order that / so that'; چه 'what' introduces the content question; گوید is the verb 'he will say' (future/optical in sense here, literary present used for future); ز کار سپاه = 'concerning the affair/work of the army'. The whole segment expresses purpose: they showed themselves so that they might hear what the king would say about the army's business.
segments.s329-g33: کنون is an adverb meaning “now” placed at the clause head. جان و دل is a coordinated noun phrase (“life and heart”) serving as the patient/object that is being separated. ز is the poetic preposition “from,” and این سرای سپنج is the prepositional object (“this spongy/‘sponge’ dwelling”) — a metaphorical locative; word order (adverb → object phrase) foregrounds the speaker’s present decision. Note: سرای’s supplied senses do not match its use here (house/dwelling), so sense is unknown in the provided list.
segments.s329-g34: بکندم is first-person singular perfective/past of کندن with the classical prefix بـ (here translated “I tore/removed”); it takes the preceding جان و دل… as its object. سرآوردم is a first-person past of سرآوردن meaning “I brought (upon myself)” or “I caused/experienced,” and it introduces the result phrase آن درد و رنج; the object (این درد و رنج) follows the verb in typical Classical Persian order. The two verbs present a sequence: I removed my life/heart, and (as a consequence) I brought this suffering.
segments.s329-g35: کنون opens the clause (“now”). آنچه جستم is a relative clause (“that which/whatever I sought”), where جستم is the classical past form of جُستن ‘to seek’ (first person). همه یافتم is the principal clause: همه (“all/everything”) is the object and یافتم is first-person past ‘I found’ — literal order mirrors Classical Persian: relative clause then the finding-statement.
segments.s329-g36: ز تخت کیی is a prepositional phrase meaning “from the Kayan (Kian) throne” (ز = from, تخت = throne, کیی = Kayanian/related to the Kian dynasty). روی is the noun “face” used with the verb برتافتم (an archaic/periphrastic past form built on بر + تافتن/رفتَن) meaning roughly “I turned (my face).” The verb is past first-person; the prepositional phrase precedes the verb as is common in older Persian.
segments.s329-g37: هر آن کس که is the standard relative phrase “whoever/anyone who.” در پیش من is a locative/prepositional phrase “in my presence” or “before me.” برد is an archaic/past form of بردن here meaning “bore/underwent” (i.e., ‘has endured’); it governs رنج (“suffering/hardship”), which is the thing endured. The clause functions as the protasis (if/relative) for the promise that follows.
segments.s329-g38: ببخشم is first-person singular present/future or subjunctive formed with the classical prefix بـ plus the verb بخشیدن; in this context it means “I will give/bestow.” بدو is the dative “to him.” هرچ خواهد ز گنج is a noun clause functioning as the object: هرچ (هر چه) = “whatever,” خواهد is third-person present of خواستن “he wants/asks,” and ز گنج = “from the treasure/treasury.” Together: “I will bestow to him whatever he asks from the treasury.”
segments.s329-g39: ز marks the reason/cause (“for, because of”). کردار هر کس که دارم is a genitive/relative construction: کردار = “deed(s),” هر کس که = “every person who,” and دارم is first-person present “I have/hold” (here meaning roughly ‘of every person I have [met/known]’ or simply linking the deeds to persons). سپاس is the object/thing to be given — “thanks” or “praise.” Together the phrase sets up the object of the following verb (to whom the thanks will be given).
segments.s329-g40: بگویم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive/future of گفتن “to say,” here meaning “I will say/offer (thanks).” به introduces the indirect object (“to/for”), یزدان is the divine addressee “God,” and نیکی‌شناس is an epithet “knower/recognizer of good” applied to God. The whole segment states the beneficiary of the speaker’s thanks.
segments.s329-g9: به آواز is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning “aloud / in a voice” (به used adverbially). گفت is the past tense verb “said.” آن زمان شهریار is the subject phrase “the king of that time/then”; in poetry the verb can precede the subject, so literal order is “aloud said then the king.”
segments.s329-g10: که‌ای here functions as an archaic/vocative exclamation (roughly “O” addressing people). نامداران is the vocative plural “renowned/illustrious ones.” به روزگار is a prepositional phrase “of/in the age” (به used in the sense “in/with respect to” + روزگار “times/age”).
segments.s329-g11: هر آن کس که is a generic relative construction meaning “whoever / any person who.” دارید is the present-tense verb “have/possess” (here addressing the audience in the 2nd‑person plural sense often used for a general ‘whoever’). راه و خرد are the coordinated objects “way (conduct) and wisdom.”
segments.s329-g12: بدانید is an imperative (or jussive) meaning “know / understand.” کاین introduces the subordinate clause (“that this…”). نیک و بد are the paired nouns “good and bad,” and بگذرد is a present/subjunctive third‑person verb “passes / will pass.” The clause states the general truth: know that good and bad pass.
segments.s329-g13: همه رفتنی‌ایم is a verbless (copular) clause with رفتنی (an adjectival/participial predicate “bound to go / transient”) plus the 1st‑person plural copula ایم: “we are all transient / we will all leave.” گیتی سپنچ is an elliptical clause “the world (is) a sponge/absorber” (copula omitted in poetic style).
segments.s329-g14: چرا باید is a rhetorical question “why should / why must.” این introduces the noun phrase “this,” and درد و اندوه و رنج are a triad “pain, sorrow, and suffering.” The main verb that would complete the question is omitted (elliptical): “why should this ... exist / be?”
segments.s329-g15: ز هر دست is a prepositional phrase “from/by every hand.” خوبی is the object “goodness / good deeds.” فراز is an adverbial “up/high / aloft” placed before the verb for emphasis, and آوریم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive “we bring / we raise.” The clause means “we bring goodness up/from every hand.”
segments.s329-g16: به دشمن بمانیم is “we remain/are left to the enemy” (به + دشمن indicating relation/recipient and بمانیم 1st‑person plural “we remain/are left”). خود بگذریم uses خود for emphasis “we ourselves” and بگذریم (1st‑person plural) meaning “we pass (away)/we go on.” The two clauses contrast where the good ends up and the fate of the speakers.
segments.s329-g49: This is a short clause with the pronoun subject شما (you) up front for emphasis, followed by the noun phrase دست شادی (literally 'hand of joy') and the prepositional phrase به خوردن ('with/to eating') that marks the sphere from which joy is severed. برید is a past tense verb (classical/past simple) meaning 'cut' or 'severed' in this poetic context; the whole clause means 'you have cut off joy's hand from eating' (a metaphor for depriving oneself or others of the pleasure of eating).
segments.s329-g50: به یک هفته is a temporal phrase 'for one week' (هفته = week / seven), ایدر is an archaic/poetic form of 'here', and چمید و چرید are two past-tense verbs joined by و ('and'). Both verbs describe light eating/browsing: چمید is a rare/archaic verb (here 'nibbled') and چرید is 'grazed/browsed'; together they mean 'they nibbled and grazed here for a week.'
segments.s329-g51: بخواهم is a first-person present/subjunctive form 'I wish' or 'I would like' and is followed by the complementizer که introducing the subordinate clause. تا here means 'until' (introducing the limit to be achieved): ز این سرای سپنج literally 'from this house/abode of sponge' — a poetic image for an absorbent or oppressive dwelling. The clause as a whole begins a desiderative subordinate clause: 'I wish that until I (can) ...'.
segments.s329-g52: گذر یابم is a subjunctive/periphrastic expression: یابم (subjunctive of یافتن) with گذر (passage) yields 'may I find a passage / may I get away.' و links the two wishes, and دور مانم ز رنج is 'I remain far/kept away from suffering' (دور = distant; مانم = subjunctive/present of ماندن 'to remain'; ز رنج = from suffering). The sequence expresses a desire to escape and to be kept away from pain.
segments.s329-g53: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'. The clause follows classical SOV order: کیخسرو (subject) + این پندها (object, 'these counsels/admonitions') + برگرفت (past verb 'took up' / 'adopted'). برگرفت is a past transitive verb meaning he accepted or adopted the advice.
segments.s329-g54: بماندند is a past-tense, third-person plural verb 'they remained/ were left.' In this line the verb precedes the noun phrase گردان ایران ('the troop/host of Iran') — an example of inverted word order for focus — and شگفت is a predicative adjective meaning 'amazed' or 'astonished.' The whole means the Iranian host remained amazed.
segments.s329-g55: یکی گفت is a conventional opening 'someone said' (یکی = someone/a certain one). کاین is an archaic fused form of که این ('that this'), here introducing reported speech: 'that this king...'. شاه دیوانه شد is a simple past clause: شاه (king) + دیوانه (mad) + شد (became) — 'this king became mad.'
segments.s329-g56: This clause states a consequence: خرد (intellect/wisdom) is the grammatical subject, با دلش ('with his heart', دل + possessive suffix ش = his) is an accompanying phrase, and سخت is an adverb 'very/strongly.' بیگانه شد is a past construction 'became alien/estranged.' Together: 'his wisdom/understanding became very alien to his heart.'

sentences.s329-s9: Now that cow lies beneath the hide, for its reward and its fleeting fortune are otherwise.
sentences.s329-s10: Fear the pure God completely; do not feel secure in this dark earth.
sentences.s329-s11: For this day too will pass over us; Fate (Time) reckons every person's moment.
sentences.s329-s12: From Hushang and Jamshid and King Kavus — who possessed glory, a throne, and a crown.
sentences.s329-s21: I will bestow upon the Iranians this requested weapon, adorned in the treasure-house.
sentences.s329-s22: To each of you who is a chief I will grant a realm.
sentences.s329-s23: Those — the moonlike beauties, the slaves, and the beasts of burden — I will consider, and I will set their numbers in their places.
sentences.s329-s24: I grant (this), for I made the road and have driven this darkness from my heart.
sentences.s329-s29: I do not know what will befall him, where this crown and throne will come to rest.
sentences.s329-s30: They departed en masse, in groups upon groups; the whole plain, the host, the bad, the desirous, and the mountain.
sentences.s329-s31: The din of the ney and the voices of the revelers from the plain — as you said — kept passing through the air.
sentences.s329-s32: They remained merrily like this for a week; no one recalled sorrow and suffering.
sentences.s329-s13: Nothing of them remained in the world except their name; no one reads the records of the departed.
sentences.s329-s14: Many of them were ungrateful; they were greatly frightened of that bad outcome.
sentences.s329-s15: Like them, I too am but one humble servant; yet even if few, I strive with hardship.
sentences.s329-s16: I tried and endured much suffering; I never saw that anyone remained here.
sentences.s329-s1: The emperor sat upon the golden throne, holding a cow‑shaped mace in his hand.
sentences.s329-s2: On one side he had Zal and Rostam together, like a proud elephant and a grim lion.
sentences.s329-s3: On the other side were Tous, Goudarz, and Giv; moreover Bijan Gard and Roham Niu.
sentences.s329-s4: They all presented their faces before the king's eye, so as to learn what he would say about the affairs of the army.
sentences.s329-s17: Now I have torn my life and heart away from this spongy dwelling; I have brought upon myself this pain and suffering.
sentences.s329-s18: Now whatever I sought I have wholly found; I have turned my face away from the Kayan (Kian) throne.
sentences.s329-s19: Whoever has endured hardship before me, I will grant him whatever he wishes from the treasure.
sentences.s329-s20: For the deeds of every person I shall give thanks to God, the knower of good.
sentences.s329-s5: At that time the king said aloud, "O renowned ones of the age,"
sentences.s329-s6: Know, whoever among you has way and wisdom, that this good and bad will pass.
sentences.s329-s7: We are all destined to go and the world is a sponge; why should there be this pain, sorrow, and suffering?
sentences.s329-s8: From every hand we bring goodness forth, yet it remains with the enemy while we ourselves pass away.
sentences.s329-s25: You cut joy's hand off from eating; for a week here they nibbled and grazed.
sentences.s329-s26: I wish that I may find passage out of this sponge-like dwelling and remain far from suffering.
sentences.s329-s27: When Kay Khosrow adopted these counsels, the Iranian host was left amazed.
sentences.s329-s28: Someone said that this king has gone mad; his reason has become very estranged from his heart.
